examples.title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
examples.description: Standard BFS algorithm for tree and graph search
examples.code:
FUNCTION BFS(problem):
 node ← MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL)
 if GOAL-TEST(problem, node.STATE) return node
 frontier ← QUEUE([node]) // FIFO queue
 explored ← SET()
 
 while frontier is not empty:
 node ← POP(frontier) // FIFO - shallowest first
 explored.add(node.STATE)
 
 for each action in problem.ACTIONS(node.STATE):
 child ← CHILD-NODE(problem, node, action)
 if child.STATE not in explored and not in frontier:
 if GOAL-TEST(problem, child.STATE):
 return child
 frontier.push(child)
 return failure
examples.explanation: Uses FIFO queue to ensure level-by-level expansion. Maintains explored set to avoid revisiting states. Returns first goal found which is optimal when costs are equal.

examples.title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
examples.description: Standard recursive DFS with cycle checking
examples.code:
FUNCTION DFS(problem):
 return RECURSIVE-DFS(MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL), SET())

FUNCTION RECURSIVE-DFS(node, explored):
 if GOAL-TEST(problem, node.STATE): return node
 explored.add(node.STATE)
 
 for each action in problem.ACTIONS(node.STATE):
 child ← CHILD-NODE(problem, node, action)
 if child.STATE not in explored:
 result ← RECURSIVE-DFS(child, explored)
 if result ≠ failure: return result
 return failure
examples.explanation: Uses recursion (stack) to go deep before backtracking. The explored set prevents cycles. Memory efficient but not complete in infinite spaces.

examples.title: Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)
examples.description: Priority queue based search by path cost
examples.code:
FUNCTION UCS(problem):
 node ← MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL)
 node.path-cost ← 0
 frontier ← PRIORITYQUEUE([node]) // Min-heap by path-cost
 explored ← SET()
 
 while frontier is not empty:
 node ← POP(frontier) // Lowest cost first
 
 if GOAL-TEST(problem, node.STATE): return node
 explored.add(node.STATE)
 
 for each action in problem.ACTIONS(node.STATE):
 child ← CHILD-NODE(problem, node, action)
 child.path-cost ← node.path-cost + action.cost
 
 if child.STATE not in explored:
 frontier.push-or-update(child)
 return failure
examples.explanation: Like Dijkstra's algorithm. Always expands node with lowest path cost. Finds optimal solution with positive costs. May explore many nodes if goal is deep.

examples.title: Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)
examples.description: Repeated DFS with increasing depth limits
examples.code:
FUNCTION IDS(problem):
 for depth = 0 to ∞:
 result ← DEPTH-LIMITED-SEARCH(problem, depth)
 if result ≠ cutoff: return result

FUNCTION DEPTH-LIMITED-SEARCH(problem, limit):
 return RECURSIVE-DLS(MAKE-NODE(problem.INITIAL), limit, SET())

FUNCTION RECURSIVE-DLS(node, limit, explored):
 if GOAL-TEST(problem, node.STATE): return node
 if limit = 0: return cutoff
 cutoff-occurred ← false
 explored.add(node.STATE)
 
 for each action in problem.ACTIONS(node.STATE):
 child ← CHILD-NODE(problem, node, action)
 if child.STATE not in explored:
 result ← RECURSIVE-DLS(child, limit-1, explored)
 if result = cutoff: cutoff-occurred ← true
 else if result ≠ failure: return result
 
 if cutoff-occurred: return cutoff
 else: return failure
examples.explanation: Combines BFS completeness with DFS memory efficiency. Each iteration is a complete DFS up to depth limit. Optimal when edge costs are equal.
